text: Imposing greater metabolic load during endurance exercise provokes extreme perturbations in cellular homeostasis that augment acute exercise responses and amplify long-term training adaptation in skeletal muscle.
Cellular adaptations to endurance training in skeletal muscle are driven by repeated bouts of exercise-induced metabolic stress accumulated over months and years, not single sessions.

Strategies that increase metabolic load during endurance exercise (such as training with low glycogen availability, heat stress, or altitude) have the potential to promote greater skeletal muscle adaptation compared to standard training conditions.
Endurance exercise activates complex intracellular signaling networks in skeletal muscle that regulate adaptation, meaning training stimulus variety matters for maximizing adaptive response.
